primary_sources.relevance: Documents mkdir, rename, rm, readFile, writeFile, exclusive creation flags, and filesystem caveats used by the prototype.
Defines the trusted-root security context in which rollback-resistant persistence and root rotation operate.

finding: A crashed writer can leave a lock indefinitely. A time-bounded owner record plus atomic rename-to-quarantine permits recovery of an expired lock while token-checked release prevents an old owner from deleting a successor lock. Incomplete or malformed lock state must fail closed rather than be stolen.

cases: expired lease is quarantined and replaced
stale owner cannot delete successor lock
missing or malformed owner record fails closed

design_implication: Integrate the lease lock around trusted-root compare-and-swap only after selecting a lease duration and clock source appropriate to the deployment. Preserve the digest compare-and-swap as the data commit condition; the lease controls writer exclusion but does not authorize root metadata.
falsification_route: Run two independent processes with forced termination at each acquisition and quarantine boundary.
Inject clock rollback and forward jumps to test whether expiry decisions can be made unsafe.
Repeat on each supported filesystem, including network filesystems, and reject unsupported rename semantics.
Demonstrate that a malformed owner record is never automatically removed.
limits: Deterministic tests used an injected clock rather than wall-clock process termination.
No distributed consensus or network-filesystem guarantee is established.
Wall-clock leases are vulnerable to clock discontinuities unless replaced or bounded by a monotonic source within one host.
The prototype does not prove authorization or cryptographic validity of the next trusted root.
